title: Equity Prevails: When Conduct Speaks Louder Than the Register of Members
description:
The Supreme Court, in Dr. Bais Surgical and Medical Institute Pvt. Ltd. & Ors. v. Dhananjay Pande, delivered a significant ruling affirming that the absence of a formal entry in the register of members does not automatically preclude a person from maintaining a petition for relief from oppression and mismanagement under Sections 397 and 398 of the Companies Act, 1956. This judgment emphasizes the primacy of equitable considerations and cumulative factual conduct over mere procedural formalities in determining locus standi.

The case involved Dhananjay Pande, who invested capital into Dr. Bais Surgical and Medical Institute. He was subsequently appointed Managing Director, and the hospital was rebranded using his trading concern's name, signifying recognition of his proprietary interest. Pande claimed shares were allotted but not formally entered in the company's register. Disputes arose, leading to his filing of company petitions alleging oppression and mismanagement.

The appellants contended that membership was exclusively defined by Section 41, requiring a formal agreement and entry in the register, and that this constituted a jurisdictional fact. Pande countered that the company's consistent conduct, accepting and utilizing his investment, appointing him MD, and renaming the hospital, unequivocally demonstrated his recognized proprietary interest, and the company should not benefit from its own default in registering his shares.

The Court critically examined Sections 2(27) and 41, concluding that the broader definition of "member" in Section 2(27) governs the locus standi for Sections 397 and 398, rather than being confined solely to the procedural requirements of Section 41. It reaffirmed the equitable character of these provisions. The Court found that Pande had been consistently recognized as a co-owner through contemporaneous correspondence, conciliation proceedings, his induction as Managing Director, and the rebranding of the hospital.

This landmark decision establishes that where a company's sustained conduct demonstrates long-standing recognition of a person's proprietary stake through correspondence, dealings, and financial arrangements, equity and justice mandate treating such person as a "member" for oppression and mismanagement proceedings, irrespective of a formal register entry. It significantly reinforces protections for minority investors and prevents companies from exploiting procedural defaults to defeat substantive shareholder rights.

title: Delegation Without Abdication : The Regulatory Architecture of SEBI’s Outsourcing Guidelines
description:
SEBI's Circular CIR/MIRSD/24/2011 comprehensively governs outsourcing by registered intermediaries, issued due to increasing reliance on third parties without adequate risk frameworks. Defining outsourcing broadly to include intra-group arrangements, the guidelines address critical risks such as loss of control, data security, business continuity, and investor protection, drawing from IOSCO principles.

The circular strictly prohibits outsourcing of core business and compliance functions, like fund management or KYC. It establishes eight binding principles. Key among these are the mandatory formulation of a comprehensive outsourcing policy by the intermediary's Board, and the establishment of a robust risk management program assessing materiality and third-party capabilities. Crucially, the guidelines assert that outsourcing does not diminish the intermediary's obligations or accountability to clients and regulators; full liability for outsourced activities remains with the intermediary, and SEBI retains unimpeded access to third-party operations.

Further principles mandate thorough due diligence on third parties, formalization of arrangements through legally binding contracts, and the implementation of business continuity and contingency plans. Intermediaries must also ensure the protection of confidential information and mitigate systemic concentration risk when a single third party serves multiple entities. Additionally, intermediaries remain responsible for reporting suspicious transactions and were directed to self-assess existing arrangements.

Legally, the framework embodies a doctrine of non-delegable accountability, meaning intermediaries cannot use third-party failures as a defense. This rigor extends to related-party outsourcing, preventing circumvention of governance requirements and extending SEBI’s supervisory perimeter. In conclusion, SEBI's guidelines ensure that operational efficiencies gained from outsourcing do not compromise market integrity or investor protection, reinforcing that delegation of functions is not an abdication of regulatory responsibility.

title: Unilateral Institutional Invocation: A Façade That Cannot Cure the Vice of Interested Party Appointment in Arbitration
description:
The Calcutta High Court, in L&T Finance Ltd. v. Jai Paras Material Store and Ors., affirmed that an interested party's unilateral invocation of an institutional mechanism cannot bypass statutory prohibitions against unilateral arbitrator appointments. Executing courts under Section 36 are empowered to refuse enforcement of awards stemming from such fundamental jurisdictional defects.

In this case, L&T Finance unilaterally referred a default dispute to Presolv360, an Online Dispute Resolution forum, for arbitrator appointment after the borrower's non-participation. An ex parte award was rendered, which L&T sought to enforce. The Court questioned the validity of an arbitration clause granting appointment power to an interested party, the legality of unilateral institutional invocation without consent, and the executing court's ability to address these fundamental issues.

The Court's analysis relied on Sections 12(5) and 14(1)(a) of the Arbitration Act, rendering interested parties ineligible and their mandates terminated. Citing Supreme Court precedents (TRF Ltd., Perkins Eastman), it underscored that an interested party cannot appoint an arbitrator, directly or indirectly, to ensure equal treatment (Section 18). Unilateral institutional invocation without mutual consent was deemed impermissible; Section 11 offers the exclusive remedy for neutral court appointment in non-participatory scenarios.

Crucially, the Court held that an award by a unilaterally appointed arbitrator is void ab initio, "non-est," lacking inherent jurisdiction. Such a fundamental defect, it affirmed, must be examined by an executing court under Section 36, irrespective of a Section 34 challenge, as no limitation cures a jurisdictional nullity. The execution petition was dismissed, the ex parte award declared unenforceable, with liberty for fresh, valid proceedings.

This judgment significantly reaffirms arbitral process integrity. It clarifies that executing courts must decline enforcement of awards suffering from such jurisdictional nullities, guiding financial institutions to utilize Section 11 court applications for non-participating respondents, rather than unilateral institutional invocation.

title: Procedural Non-Compliance and Limitation Defeat Bank’s SARFAESI Action: An Analysis of the Gopu Bala Reddy v. Union Bank of India Ruling
description:
The Debt Recovery Tribunal, Hyderabad, in `Gopu Bala Reddy v. Union Bank of India`, underscored the mandatory nature of procedural compliance and limitation periods under the SARFAESI Act. The ruling invalidated Union Bank of India's enforcement actions against guarantors for a loan, citing multiple legal breaches.

Crucially, the Tribunal found the bank failed to serve the principal borrower (M/s BRG Energy Limited, undergoing liquidation) with the mandatory Section 13(2) demand notice. This rendered subsequent measures void ab initio, emphasizing that borrower notification is essential even when pursuing guarantors.

Further procedural infirmities included non-compliance with Rule 8 of the Security Interest Rules for possession and sale notices. The bank published in an obscure newspaper, failed to provide proof of affixture for notices, and lacked valuation reports for the property, raising concerns of potential collusion due to the sole bidder.

Significantly, the Tribunal upheld the limitation defense. The loan was classified as NPA on December 31, 2011. The 12-year period under Article 62 of the Limitation Act for mortgage enforcement expired on December 31, 2023. Thus, the bank's fresh demand notice in March 2024 was time-barred under Section 36 of SARFAESI, rejecting COVID-19 exclusion arguments.

The DRT allowed the application, declaring the bank's measures void, setting aside the sale, and ordering a refund. This judgment is pivotal, reinforcing that SARFAESI powers are not unbridled and demand strict adherence to statutory procedures and limitation, profoundly influencing future enforcement strategies for secured creditors.

title: Eureka Forbes Ltd. v. Indian Railway Catering and Tourism Corporation: Reaffirming Fairness in Arbitral Proceedings
description:
The Delhi High Court, in Eureka Forbes Ltd. v. Indian Railway Catering and Tourism Corporation, reaffirmed that arbitral tribunals' procedural discretion must align with natural justice and fairness. The judgment critically examined the treatment of counterclaims when a party is proceeded against ex parte and clarified the distinction between a procedural order and an interim arbitral award.

The dispute arose from a Licence Agreement, leading to arbitration where Eureka Forbes filed a counterclaim. Following Eureka Forbes' non-appearance at two consecutive hearings, the Arbitrator proceeded ex parte against the company and rejected its counterclaim. He subsequently refused to restore the counterclaim, deeming its rejection a final determination beyond his jurisdiction.

Eureka Forbes challenged these orders under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The High Court determined that the ex parte order was merely procedural and thus not challengeable as an interim award. However, applying a threefold test (final adjudication of a substantive claim, binding consequences, and the tribunal becoming functus officio regarding that issue), the Court concluded that the rejection of the counterclaim was an interim award. This rejection effectively brought a substantive claim to an end without adjudication on merits, violating statutory principles of fairness and equal treatment (Sections 18 and 25 of the Act).

The Court emphasized that mere non-appearance does not automatically justify dismissing an already filed counterclaim. Such claims should be adjudicated on available material, as procedural defaults should not lead to the forfeiture of substantive rights. Setting aside the interim award, the Delhi High Court restored the counterclaim for adjudication, thereby reinforcing that arbitration must uphold fairness, equality, and due process, ensuring meaningful access to justice.

title: Writ or Tribunal? The SARFAESI Crossroads for Auction Purchasers
description:
The Punjab and Haryana High Court, in Devi Hospitality Pvt. Ltd. v. Punjab National Bank & Anr., has definitively ruled that the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) under Section 17(1) of the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 (SARFAESI Act), is the exclusive and efficacious forum for aggrieved auction purchasers, to the exclusion of writ jurisdiction under Article 226 of the Constitution of India.

The petitioner, Devi Hospitality, emerged as the highest bidder in a PNB e-auction but encountered issues with the "Sale Confirmation Letter," citing deficiencies and subsequent allegations of fraudulent documentation by the bank. Despite depositing 25% of the bid amount, the petitioner sought an extension for the balance payment due to the COVID-19 pandemic. PNB, however, cancelled the sale and forfeited the Rs. 1.07 crore deposit, leading the petitioner to file a writ petition.

PNB contended that the writ petition was not maintainable, citing Supreme Court precedents like Agarwal Tracom Pvt. Ltd. v. Punjab National Bank. This case established that the forfeiture of an auction purchaser's deposit under SARFAESI Rules is an integral measure under Section 13(4) of the Act, challengeable solely before the DRT. The High Court, observing factual similarities to Agarwal Tracom, declined to adjudicate disputed facts in writ jurisdiction. It disposed of the petition, directing Devi Hospitality to approach the jurisdictional DRT within 45 days. The DRT was instructed to consider the application on its merits without dismissing it on grounds of limitation.

This judgment reinforces that the DRT is the designated and exclusive forum for challenging actions taken under the SARFAESI Act, including the forfeiture of deposits. It cautions against invoking extraordinary writ jurisdiction without first exhausting the available statutory remedies, which can lead to procedural delays and complications.

title: Acquire the Land, Pay the Price: Madhya Pradesh High Court Reaffirms Timely Compensation as a Constitutional Right
description:
The Madhya Pradesh High Court, in a significant ruling in Shanti Singh & Others v. State of Madhya Pradesh & Others, reaffirmed that the right to receive compensation for acquired land is an essential component of the constitutional guarantee under Article 300-A. The Court emphasized that once land acquisition is completed and compensation duly determined, governmental authorities cannot indefinitely withhold payment on the pretext of procedural or administrative delays.

The dispute involved land acquired for a National Highway, where compensation of ₹3,35,40,000/- was awarded but remained unpaid despite the land being utilized. The Court rejected the State's argument of pending administrative formalities, asserting that bureaucratic delays cannot justify depriving landowners of compensation that has been adjudicated. It highlighted that arbitrary delays violate both Article 300-A and Article 14, undermining the rule of law and the integrity of the acquisition process.

The judgment underscores that fair and timely compensation is not merely a statutory benefit but an indispensable component of lawful acquisition, especially for policies like "missing plots" acquired through mutual consent. The High Court directed immediate disbursement of the compensation, preferably within eight weeks. This ruling reinforces constitutional safeguards, ensuring that developmental objectives do not override individual property rights and demanding greater administrative accountability in land acquisition processes.

title: No Coparcenary, No Karta: Supreme Court Rewrites the Conversation on Inherited Property
description:
The Supreme Court in Darubai & Anr. v. Kamalabai & Ors. reaffirmed a foundational principle: property inherited by Class I heirs under Section 8 of the Hindu Succession Act, 1956 (HSA) devolves as separate property, held as tenants-in-common, not as joint family or coparcenary property. Consequently, no heir can assume the role of 'karta' or unilaterally alienate the entire estate based on 'legal necessity' beyond their defined share.

This ruling stemmed from a five-decade dispute involving a widow who alienated inherited land, asserting karta authority. The SC, analyzing Sections 8, 10, and 19 of the HSA, clarified that statutory succession creates distinct, ascertainable shares, moving away from traditional Mitakshara survivorship. Each heir acquires an independent interest, making collective management doctrines inapplicable unless a coparcenary is otherwise proven.

The decision strengthens the HSA's statutory scheme, ensuring individual ownership rights override customary concepts inconsistent with it. It provides crucial guidance for property disputes, reiterating that property originating from a common ancestor does not automatically become joint family property after 1956 statutory succession. This promotes certainty in property relations and emphasizes due diligence in transactions involving inherited assets.

title: Dignity in Custody: Rajasthan High Court Condemns Custodial Humiliation as Unlawful Extrajudicial Punishment
description:
In a landmark ruling, the Rajasthan High Court, in Puranmal v. State of Rajasthan & Ors. (27 May 2026), unequivocally condemned the forcible public parading of an accused, cross-dressing, and the subsequent circulation of their photographs on social media as an unlawful form of extrajudicial punishment. The petitioner alleged that police officials subjected him to forced head-shaving, dressed him in women's clothing, and paraded him through a marketplace before his formal court production, causing grave and irreparable harm to his dignity and fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution.

The Court rejected the police's explanation—that the accused was already in disguise and photos were taken by third parties—as inconsistent with ordinary human conduct and inherently implausible. Justice Farjand Ali underscored that such police conduct reflects a profound overreach of authority, infringing upon the core tenets of human dignity and constitutional morality. The judgment reaffirmed that the police, as an executive arm, are confined to law enforcement and public order, not adjudicating guilt or imposing punishments, which are exclusively judicial functions.

The High Court expressed deep concern over the increasing trend of "media trials by police," including "perp walks" and publicizing accused persons' images, which prematurely declare guilt and undermine the presumption of innocence. Citing Supreme Court precedents and the Law Commission's report on "Trial by Media," the Court emphasized that such actions inflict permanent digital scars, violating the right to live with dignity under Article 21. It clarified that Indian penal statutes exhaustively define lawful punishments, none of which include public shaming or media exposure; therefore, police-orchestrated humiliation constitutes an "extra-legal penalty."

To curb such practices, the Court issued general directions prohibiting police from compelling accused individuals to pose for media, publishing their photos/videos on social media or in the press, or making statements that insinuate guilt. It further mandated the prominent display of "Do’s and Don’ts" guidelines regarding custodial conduct and media interaction at all police stations and departmental web portals across Rajasthan. While refraining from immediate punitive action against the concerned officials, the Court reserved the petitioner's liberty to pursue legal remedies for damages, reinforcing the systemic importance of upholding custodial dignity and constitutional principles against police overreach.

title: Supreme Court Orders Time-Bound Removal of Forest Encroachments in Agasthyamalai Landscape
description:
The Supreme Court, in A. John Kennedy & Others v. State of Tamil Nadu, has issued a significant ruling mandating the time-bound removal of extensive forest encroachments within the Agasthyamalai landscape. This region, encompassing the Kalakad-Mundanthurai and Srivilliputhur-Megamalai Tiger Reserves and Kanyakumari Wildlife Sanctuary, is a globally recognized biodiversity hotspot. The Court observed decades of administrative inaction, inadequate enforcement, and widespread illegal occupation, including by serving and retired government officials, despite the State's partial rehabilitation efforts.

The judgment firmly reiterates environmental protection as a constitutional imperative, deriving from Articles 21, 48A, and 51A(g), and adopts an ecocentric approach, acknowledging the intrinsic value of nature. While stressing the necessity of rehabilitation for affected occupants, the Court clarified that humanitarian considerations cannot indefinitely defer the removal of encroachments from ecologically sensitive areas. It emphasized that conservation and rehabilitation are complementary, not mutually exclusive, objectives.

To ensure effective restoration, the Supreme Court issued comprehensive directives including a six-month deadline for the eviction of ineligible encroachers, a one-year timeframe for addressing issues related to the Bombay Burmah Trading Corporation Limited (BBTCL) leased lands, and the completion of surveys, demarcation, and rehabilitation schemes within specific periods. It also mandated disciplinary and legal action against implicated public officials and ordered continued judicial supervision through periodic status reports. This landmark decision reinforces institutional accountability, sustainable governance, and the judiciary's commitment to preserving critical ecosystems.

title: Determination of Financial versus Operational Debt in Invoice Financing Transactions Under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016
description:
The NCLT Amaravati, in Kushal Finnovation Capital Pvt. Ltd. v. Rajesh Chillale, addressed critical questions regarding the classification of invoice financing as financial or operational debt and the validity of unregistered security interests during the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP). Kushal Finnovation Capital Private Limited provided "Invoice Financing–Dual Borrower" facilities to cotton suppliers, with Kallam Brothers Cottons Private Limited (Corporate Debtor) acting as guarantor.

Kushal Finnovation initially filed a claim for Rs. 5.33 Crore, admitted as an unsecured financial creditor. It subsequently sought reclassification as a secured financial creditor, relying on a general lien clause (Clause 5.1) in the credit agreements. However, the Interim Resolution Professional (IRP) rejected this request, asserting the debt was operational (stemming from bill discounting for goods purchased by the CD) and that no valid, registered security interest existed, especially considering prior registered charges held by other banks.

The Tribunal framed two pivotal issues. On debt classification, it determined that since Kushal Finnovation's recovery was confined to specific financed assets (cotton and related receivables), the facility was effectively "non-recourse." Consequently, it did not fall under Section 5(8)(e) of the IBC as financial debt. Instead, viewing its true commercial substance as the discounting of trade invoices, the Tribunal classified it as operational debt under Section 5(21). Regarding security interest, the Tribunal found Clause 5.1, a general lien over unspecified assets, insufficient to create an identifiable, enforceable charge without proper registration, which serves as crucial public notice.

Ultimately, the NCLT dismissed the application, deferring the reclassification decision to the IRP. This judgment reinforces that commercial substance takes precedence over contractual nomenclature. It clarifies that non-recourse invoice financing constitutes operational debt and that secured creditor status cannot be claimed through unregistered, unperfected, or broadly worded contractual liens. Financiers in the trade finance ecosystem must ensure security interests are properly documented and registered to secure their claims during corporate insolvency.

title: Qurban Without Cows: The Madras High Court Reaffirms the Absolute Bar on Cow Slaughter in Tamil Nadu
description:
In a significant pronouncement on 27 May 2026, the Madras High Court's Division Bench, comprising Justices G.R. Swaminathan and V. Lakshminarayanan, allowed a Public Interest Litigation directing the State of Tamil Nadu to ensure no cow or calf is slaughtered on Bakrid or any other day. The judgment, delivered in K. Surya alias K. Surya Prasanth v. Secretary to the Government & Ors., comprehensively reaffirms the absolute legal prohibition on bovine slaughter in the state.

The petitioner, K. Surya Prasanth, sought a Writ of Mandamus against arrangements for cow and calf sacrifices in temporary sheds for Bakrid. The Court anchored its decision in Article 48 of the Constitution, which directs the State to prohibit the slaughter of cows, calves, and other milch and draught cattle. Statutorily, it relied on Section 4 of the Tamil Nadu Animal Preservation Act, 1958, strictly mandating that an animal must be both over ten years of age AND unfit for work or breeding for slaughter to be permissible. Furthermore, G.O. Ms. No. 1715, banning cow and heifer slaughter in all slaughterhouses, was held to have the force of law.

The judgment drew upon established Supreme Court precedents, including Mohammed Hanif Quareshi v. State of Bihar (1958) and State of West Bengal v. Ashutosh Lahiri (1995), which clarified that cow sacrifice is not an obligatory or essential religious practice for Muslims. The Court also cited State of Gujarat v. Mirzapur Moti Kureshi Kassab Jamat (2005), which upheld the constitutional validity of a complete ban on the slaughter of the progeny of cows. Crucially, the Court emphasized that Rule 298 of the Tamil Nadu Urban Local Bodies Rules, 2023, mandates slaughter exclusively in designated and licensed slaughterhouses, expressly prohibiting temporary sheds.

The Madras High Court issued sweeping directives for the Chief Secretary and the Additional Director General of Police (Law and Order) to ensure statewide compliance, requiring a report by 29 May 2026. This landmark ruling reinforces the justiciability of Directive Principles, the binding nature of executive orders with legislative co-extensive power, and unequivocally establishes that no religious occasion can override clear statutory prohibitions, thereby upholding the supremacy of the rule of law.

title: Regulatory Developments in Food Product Standards
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has published a draft notification dated May 21, 2026, proposing amendments to the Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011. Stakeholders are invited to submit objections or suggestions within 60 days, approximately until July 24, 2026.

The first key amendment introduces a new sub-regulation 2.2.10 for "Minor Seed Oils," including chilli, muskmelon, okra, and tomato seed oils. It establishes specific quality parameters for cold-pressed/virgin and refined oils, covering aspects like volatile matter, insoluble impurities, soap content, acid value, and heavy metals (iron, copper). Hexane-extracted oils must be refined, adhering to a 5.0 mg/kg hexane residue limit, while pressed oils must be hexane-free. Food additives are only permissible in refined minor seed oils.

Secondly, a new sub-regulation 2.3.66 defines "Edible seeds" for direct human consumption, encompassing both vegetable (e.g., watermelon, muskmelon, pumpkin) and oilseeds (e.g., sunflower, sesame, flaxseed). Proposed quality parameters include maximum limits for moisture (8.00%), free fatty acids (1.25% on dry mass), and foreign articles (0.1%).

Finally, the draft amends Appendix A, Table 14, to permit the use of potassium polyaspartate (INS No. 456) at a maximum level of 100 mg/kg in grape wines (Food Category System 14.2.3). This aligns the Indian framework with evolving international oenological practices.

These amendments aim to address existing regulatory gaps, enhance consumer protection, and provide greater clarity for food business operators.

title: FSSAI Notifies Amendment Regulations on Contaminants, Toxins and Residues: Expanded Scope for Pulse Flours, Seafood Residue Limits, and Arsenic Testing Protocols
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has published the Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Amendment Regulations, 2026, effective December 1, 2026. Notified on May 14, 2026, these amendments update the 2011 regulations, following a public consultation without objections. The revisions aim to fortify food safety standards across three domains: metal contaminants, crop contaminants and naturally occurring toxic substances, and residues of veterinary drugs and antibiotics.

In metal contaminants, the maximum levels for lead and cadmium have been extended to "Pulses and Pulse flours," ensuring consistent regulatory scrutiny. A significant amendment for arsenic in fish oils introduces a maximum level of 0.1 mg/kg for inorganic arsenic (As-in) and a two-tier testing protocol. This requires initial total arsenic screening, with specific inorganic arsenic testing only if the total arsenic exceeds the permissible inorganic limit.

Amendments for crop contaminants refine product descriptions for total aflatoxins, clearly distinguishing between oils and ready-to-eat oilseeds. The saffrole limit of 10 is now explicitly applied to "Beverages and foods containing mace and/or nutmeg kernel as an ingredient," enhancing specificity. Furthermore, new residue limits address veterinary drugs in seafood: trimethoprim at 0.05 and oxolinic acid at 0.3 for "Sea foods including shrimps, prawns or any other variety of fish and fishery products."

The regulations provide a six-month transition period until December 1, 2026, enabling food business operators, testing laboratories, and enforcement agencies to align their protocols and product specifications. These comprehensive amendments represent a substantive advancement in India's food safety regime, aligning with evolving scientific understanding and global best practices.

title: Single Judge Designated as Commercial Court Cannot Be Treated as Lacking Jurisdiction Merely for Non-Transfer Under Section 15 of Commercial Courts Act: Supreme Court
description:
The Supreme Court has ruled that a judgment passed by a Single Judge, who was designated as a Commercial Court, cannot be deemed a nullity or without jurisdiction solely because a Section 34 Arbitration and Conciliation Act petition was not formally transferred under Section 15 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. This decision reversed a Calcutta High Court Division Bench order that had set aside a Single Judge's decision only on jurisdictional grounds, without examining the merits of the appeal.

The case originated from Steel Authority of India Ltd.'s (SAIL) challenge to an arbitration award via a Section 34 petition. During the petition's pendency, the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, came into force. The Single Judge subsequently dismissed SAIL's petition. The Division Bench later set aside this dismissal, arguing that the petition ought to have been transferred to the Commercial Division, thus rendering the Single Judge's order jurisdictionally flawed.

The Supreme Court, however, found that the Single Judge was, in fact, designated as a Commercial Court, as evidenced by the cause list. Crucially, SAIL had participated in the proceedings without objection and had not availed itself of Section 15(5) of the 2015 Act, which allows parties to seek transfer of such matters. The Court clarified that while a bench acting without proper assignment is a nullity, this principle did not apply here as the judge was appropriately designated. The lack of formal transfer was deemed a procedural or administrative irregularity, not an inherent jurisdictional defect.

Consequently, the Supreme Court quashed the Division Bench's order and remitted the matter to the Commercial Appellate Division of the Calcutta High Court to hear and decide the Section 37 appeal on its merits afresh.

title: Possession Without Protest Is No Waiver
description:
In a significant ruling on May 6, 2026, the Uttar Pradesh Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (UP REAT), in Uttar Pradesh Awas Vikas Parishad v. Real Estate Regulatory Authority, Lucknow & Ors., affirmed that a promoter’s obligation to pay delay interest under Section 18(1) of the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA) is absolute, mandatory, and cannot be waived by an allottee accepting possession without formal protest.

The case involved an allottee who received flat possession from the Uttar Pradesh Awas Vikas Parishad over three years beyond the stipulated completion date. After an Adjudicating Officer (AO) granted delay interest, the promoter appealed, contending the AO lacked jurisdiction for such claims and that the allottee, by accepting possession without protest, had implicitly waived their right to delay interest.

The Tribunal concurred that the AO lacked jurisdiction, setting aside the impugned order. However, it utilized its powers to provide complete justice, directly addressing the substantive issue of waiver. UP REAT unequivocally rejected the promoter's waiver argument, emphasizing that the "shall" in Section 18(1) makes the payment of delay interest a self-executing, absolute right for the allottee. Accepting possession, without more, does not extinguish this statutory entitlement, as doing so would undermine RERA's protective framework and incentivize promoters to delay.

Ultimately, the Tribunal directed the promoter to pay delay interest at MCLR+1% per month on the deposited amount for the delayed period (August 27, 2015, to August 31, 2017) within 45 days. This judgment significantly reinforces homebuyer rights, establishing that delay interest under RERA is a mandatory and mathematical entitlement, not a discretionary concession subject to implicit waiver through the acceptance of possession.

title: Life Beyond Life: How the Supreme Court Granted Freedom to a Man Who Served Over Two Decades
description:
In a significant ruling, the Supreme Court of India in Munna Moyuddin Shaikh v. State of Gujarat (2026 INSC 558) exercised its inherent power to modify a sentence of life imprisonment. The Court reduced the sentence of Munna Moyuddin Shaikh, convicted for murder under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code, to the period he had already served—over twenty-three years behind bars. This judgment, delivered by Justices K. V. Viswanathan and Vijay Bishnoi, clarified the scope of modified sentencing by constitutional courts.

Shaikh was convicted in 1998 by a Trial Court, a decision upheld by the Gujarat High Court in 2002. While the conviction itself was not seriously challenged before the Supreme Court, the core legal question revolved around the power of constitutional courts to modify a life sentence to a fixed term corresponding to the period already undergone, and whether this required prior notice to the accused.

The Court’s analysis drew upon crucial precedents: Union of India v. V. Sriharan affirmed that life imprisonment generally means natural life, but constitutional courts (High Court or Supreme Court) possess the power to impose modified fixed-term sentences. Shiva Kumar v. State of Karnataka expanded on this, stating that modifying a life sentence to a fixed term (not less than fourteen years) is a general sentencing tool, not restricted to cases involving the death penalty. Furthermore, Birbal Choudhary v. State of Bihar clarified that such modification constitutes a reduction, not an enhancement, of the sentence, thus obviating the need for prior notice under the Criminal Procedure Code.

Considering the appellant’s age at the time of the 1998 incident (approximately 21 years) and his actual imprisonment exceeding two decades, the Supreme Court upheld the concurrent findings on guilt. However, exercising its discretion, the Bench directed that the life sentence be reduced to the 23 years, 6 months, and 3 days already served, ordering Shaikh's immediate release unless required in other cases. This decision underscores the Supreme Court's role in ensuring humane and proportionate justice through a sensitive exercise of its wide sentencing powers.

title: Kerala High Court Clarifies Limits of Intermediary Liability and Writ Jurisdiction in Online Defamation Cases
description:
The Kerala High Court, in a significant judgment on May 22, 2026, in Anto Augustine v. Union of India, has delineated the boundaries of intermediary liability and writ jurisdiction regarding online defamation. The Court dismissed a petition seeking the removal of allegedly defamatory content on YouTube and compensation, asserting that intermediaries cannot be compelled to remove such content without a prior judicial declaration of its unlawful nature.

The ruling affirmed YouTube's status as an "intermediary" under the Information Technology Act, 2000, entitling it to safe harbour protections. Crucially, the Court reiterated the Supreme Court's Shreya Singhal precedent, codifying that "actual knowledge" triggering an intermediary's obligation to remove unlawful content arises solely upon receiving a court order or an authorized government notification, not merely from a private complaint or legal notice. The Court highlighted the legislative intent behind the removal of "defamatory" from the IT Rules, 2021, acknowledging that private intermediaries are institutionally incapable of adjudicating complex defamation claims.

Furthermore, the judgment underscored that Article 226 writ jurisdiction is unsuitable for fact-intensive defamation cases, which necessitate a detailed adjudication of falsity, malice, and harm—issues best resolved by civil courts that can provide remedies such as injunctive relief and damages. Free speech considerations also mandate a prior judicial finding of defamation before content can be restricted. A writ of mandamus against government authorities was also denied, as the petitioners had not formally approached the ministries with specific allegations of IT Rules non-compliance.

Practically, this judgment mandates that individuals aggrieved by online defamation must pursue civil litigation to obtain a court order, which then activates the intermediary's obligation to remove the content expeditiously. For digital intermediaries, the ruling reinforces their safe harbour, requiring action only upon judicial or authorized governmental directives, while emphasizing continuous compliance with other IT Rules. Regulators are expected to be engaged formally by stakeholders before courts intervene in enforcement matters.

This landmark ruling consolidates the intermediary liability framework, resisting the expansion of writ jurisdiction into contested factual determinations and reinforcing that private complaints alone are insufficient to displace intermediary safe harbour protections.

title: The Interplay of Commercial Consistency and Undervalued Transactions: An Analysis of the NCLT Kochi Bench Judgment in Piyush Kisanlal Jani v. Chuzattil Naryanana Manoj
description:
The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) Kochi Bench recently dismissed an application by the Resolution Professional (RP) of Pelican Biotech and Chemicals Labs Private Limited, alleging undervalued transactions by its suspended directors. The RP’s audit report claimed the corporate debtor sold "COMPOSORB 100 LTR" to a related entity, M/s Pelican Kenterra Private Limited, at Rs. 300 per unit, significantly lower than the Rs. 862 per unit charged to third parties, resulting in a Rs. 5.56 lakh loss and violating IBC Section 45. The RP contended this price differential demonstrated an intent to confer undue benefit on a related party.

The suspended directors countered on several grounds. Procedurally, they argued the application was time-barred due to a 164-day delay, bypassing Regulation 35A, and challenged the non-joinder of M/s Pelican Kenterra Private Limited as a necessary party. Substantively, they claimed the transactions were arm's length, justified by their intellectual property rights and a royalty arrangement benefiting the corporate debtor. Crucially, they highlighted that the RP himself continued supplying the same product to M/s Pelican Kenterra Private Limited at the identical Rs. 300-400 per unit price during the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP).

The NCLT dismissed the limitation objection, ruling that Regulation 35A timelines are directory, not mandatory, and a procedural delay cannot extinguish substantive rights. The non-joinder plea was also rejected, with the Tribunal classifying M/s Pelican Kenterra as a proper, not necessary, party since no direct adverse order was sought against it. On the merits, the NCLT found the RP’s continued adoption of the same pricing model post-CIRP compellingly negated the undervaluation claim. This commercial consistency indicated a legitimate, long-standing business arrangement rather than deliberate asset stripping.

The Tribunal concluded that the continuous transaction pattern disproved any intent to prejudice creditors. This judgment reinforces that avoidance applications cannot be based solely on theoretical price variances; commercial realities, consistent pricing structures, and independent business judgments, particularly when adopted by the RP himself, must be holistically evaluated before declaring transactions as undervalued under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code.

title: Survival of Personal Guarantors’ Liability Post-Corporate Resolution Plan: An Analysis of the NCLAT Ruling in Amit Bhatnagar v. UCO Bank
description:
The NCLAT in Amit Bhatnagar v. UCO Bank provided critical clarity on personal guarantors' liability under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016. The ruling affirmed that such obligations can survive the approval of a resolution plan for a corporate guarantor, addressing the maintainability of Section 95 proceedings concerning limitation and the extinguishment of independent guarantor liability.

The case stemmed from personal guarantees securing credit facilities to a Corporate Debtor. Following the Corporate Debtor's liquidation and a DRT decree establishing the debt against both the Corporate Debtor and the personal guarantors, the corporate guarantor underwent its own Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP). A resolution plan for the corporate guarantor was subsequently approved. Although a consortium bank accepted payment under this plan as "full and final" for its claims within that specific CIRP, it later initiated Section 95 IBC proceedings against the personal guarantors for the outstanding balance.

The Appellants (personal guarantors) contended that the debt was extinguished by the corporate guarantor's resolution plan and the "full and final" settlement letter. They also argued the claims were time-barred from the original default date. Conversely, the banks maintained that personal guarantees were distinct, the settlement was limited to the corporate guarantor's CIRP, and the DRT decree provided a fresh, extended limitation period.

The NCLAT dismissed the appeals, upholding the NCLT's admission of the Section 95 petitions. It held that the DRT decree of February 6, 2020, served as an acknowledgment of debt, providing a fresh cause of action, which, combined with the Supreme Court's COVID-19 extension, rendered the Section 95 petitions timely. Crucially, relying on Lalit Kumar Jain v. Union of India, the Tribunal reiterated that a resolution plan's approval does not discharge a personal guarantor's liability. Partial recovery or settlement with one obligor does not extinguish the co-extensive liability of other obligors.

This judgment reinforces the enduring and independent nature of personal guarantee enforcement within India's insolvency framework, confirming that creditors retain the right to pursue guarantors for unpaid balances even after a corporate resolution.

title: Supreme Court Reaffirms Equitable Powers Under Section 28 of the Specific Relief Act: No Automatic Rescission for Delay in Deposit
description:
In Anand Narayan Shukla v. Jagat Dhari, the Supreme Court significantly reaffirmed the equitable nature of specific performance decrees under Section 28 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963. The Court clarified that a decree for specific performance does not automatically become inexecutable merely due to a purchaser's delay in depositing the balance sale consideration. Courts retain continuing supervisory jurisdiction and must adopt a justice-oriented, equitable approach, as rescission under Section 28 is discretionary, not automatic.

The case arose when an appellant (purchaser), despite a Trial Court decree to deposit consideration within one month, faced delays. This was due to the respondent's non-cooperation, their appeal (without a stay), and varying directions from the Execution Court. The appellant deposited the required amount upon explicit direction, yet lower courts rescinded the contract based on the initial delay.

The Supreme Court stressed that a specific performance decree is preliminary, keeping the court's jurisdiction active. Section 28 embodies equitable principles, mandating an evaluation of circumstances, parties' conduct, and bona fides. The Court criticized the lower courts' rigid application, noting the appellant's consistent readiness and willingness versus the respondent's actions and procedural ambiguities.

The ruling establishes key principles: courts possess ongoing power to extend time even after the expiry of the stipulated period, focusing on the decree-holder's wilful negligence rather than strict adherence to limitation laws. A holistic, equitable review is paramount. The Supreme Court overturned the lower courts' decisions, restoring the matter for fresh adjudication to ensure substantive justice prevails over hyper-technical interpretations.

title: Supreme Court: Residential Sale Deed of Adjoining Village Cannot Determine Compensation for Industrial Land Acquisition
description:
The Supreme Court has decisively ruled that a residential sale deed for land in an adjoining village cannot be used to determine compensation for industrial land acquired under the Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013 (2013 Act). The Court underscored the mandatory nature of Section 26(1)(b) of the 2013 Act, which requires compensation to be based on "similar type of land," clarifying that industrial and residential properties are not comparable.

This decision arose from the acquisition of 1,394 square metres of Alfa Remidis Ltd.'s land in Nagpur, used for industrial purposes, for a National Highway. The Arbitrator had mistakenly relied on a residential sale deed from an adjacent village, valuing the land at ₹3,588 per square metre, a valuation upheld by the Bombay High Court.

The Supreme Court, allowing the appeal, found that both the Arbitrator and High Court failed to adhere to the statutory mandate. It reiterated that determining the "average sale price" under Section 26(1)(b) necessitates considering multiple sale deeds, not a single transaction. Consequently, the Court rejected the residential sale deed benchmark and instead adopted the Ready Reckoner valuation of ₹2,020 per square metre as the appropriate compensation, in addition to all statutory benefits. This judgment reinforces the principle that land acquisition compensation must align with comparable transactions involving lands of similar nature and use to ensure fairness and consistency.

title: Delegation Of Powers Under The Industrial Relations Code, 2020: An Analysis Of The Gazette Notification Dated 8th May, 2026
description:
The Industrial Relations Code, 2020 (IRC), effective November 21, 2025, consolidated three foundational labour statutes in India. On May 8, 2026, the Ministry of Labour and Employment issued Gazette Notification S.O. 2323(E), signifying a key administrative step by delegating Central Government powers under the IRC.

Utilizing Section 100 of the IRC, the notification delegates powers concerning Sections 78, 79, and 80 to Joint Secretary-level officers and above within the Ministry's Industrial Relations portfolio. This delegation mechanism is crucial for efficient, expert-level administration given India's industrial scale, ensuring consequential decisions are made by senior, accountable officers, without diluting statutory safeguards.

The delegated powers fall under Chapter X of the Code, applying to non-seasonal establishments employing 300+ workers. These provisions require prior government permission for lay-off (Section 78), set conditions for worker retrenchment (Section 79), and govern undertaking closure (Section 80).

Practically, the notification clarifies the competent authority for establishments under Central Government jurisdiction, streamlining the process by identifying specific senior officers for applications under these crucial sections. It also provides clarity for workers and unions on the designated officials for required hearings. This administrative measure is vital for the IRC's effective implementation, ensuring that decisions on matters significantly impacting employers and workers are handled by experienced, senior personnel, without altering substantive legal requirements.

title: RBI Overhauls the Authorised Person Framework: A Close Reading of the Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026
description:
The Reserve Bank of India has introduced the Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026, marking a significant overhaul of the foreign exchange licensing framework. These regulations, effective from April 30, 2026, replace the prior regime, aiming for structural rationalisation, enhanced regulatory oversight, and improved procedural fairness.

Key changes include a clearer, three-tier categorisation of Authorised Dealers (ADs) and a separate class for Full Fledged Money Changers (FFMCs). AD Category-I is for banks with the broadest scope. AD Category-II encompasses banks, NBFCs, and eligible FFMCs/Forex Correspondents (FxCs), handling specific non-trade current account transactions and foreign trade up to Rs. 25 lakh. AD Category-III is an innovation-enabling category for entities dealing in foreign exchange incidental to their business or offering novel services, with tailored authorisation.

The new regulations introduce stricter eligibility criteria. Applicants, if not RBI-regulated, must be companies with minimum net worth (Rs. 10 crore for AD Cat-II, Rs. 2 crore for AD Cat-III). A "fit and proper" standard applies to the entity, its promoters, directors, and Key Managerial Personnel, requiring at least 50% with financial services experience. Disclosure of Directorate of Enforcement (DoE) investigations is mandatory, with a deemed-compliance mechanism for DoE inaction. Notably, no new FFMC authorisations will be granted; existing FFMCs must meet revised net worth criteria for renewal.

Ongoing conditions for authorisation include maintaining minimum annual forex turnover, restoring net worth within six months if it falls, and seeking RBI approval for significant changes in management or ownership for non-bank entities. Digital integration is paramount, with the PRAVAAH platform for applications and APConnect for real-time reporting of operational changes and events.

A formal appeal mechanism has been established, allowing applicants or authorised persons to appeal rejections or revocations to the Executive Director of the Foreign Exchange Department within 45 days, with a reasoned order expected within 60 days.

Furthermore, the existing franchisee model for money changing will be phased out within two years, replaced by the structured Forex Correspondent Scheme (FCS). Under this scheme, AD Category-I or AD Category-II entities can appoint FxCs under a principal-agent model, with principals required to formulate Board-approved policies ensuring governance and accountability.

These Regulations necessitate immediate review by all stakeholders. While largely consolidatory for established AD Category-I banks, NBFCs and smaller AD Category-II entities must assess their compliance with net worth and governance requirements. Existing FFMCs must plan for renewal or transition to the FxC model. The AD Category-III route offers an avenue for innovative market entrants, subject to stringent eligibility. The framework aims for a more digitally integrated, transparent, and accountable foreign exchange market.

title: Dolly Khilankumar Vadalia v. Union of India: Citizenship by Descent and Consular Registration
description:
The High Court of Gujarat, in Dolly Khilankumar Vadalia v. Union of India, dismissed a writ petition seeking an Indian passport, emphasizing the mandatory nature of consular birth registration for claiming Indian citizenship by descent. The petitioner, born in Mozambique in 2000 to Indian citizen parents, returned to India weeks after birth due to floods and has since resided continuously in India, holding multiple Indian identity documents. She never acquired foreign nationality.

Her application for an Indian passport was rejected due to her inability to provide proof of Indian citizenship, specifically the lack of consular registration of her birth within one year, as mandated by Section 4(1)(b) of the Citizenship Act, 1955. The petitioner argued that insisting on consular registration in her unique circumstances violated her fundamental rights under Articles 14 and 21, asserting that her integration into Indian society and possession of Indian identity documents should suffice.

However, the respondents maintained that the statutory requirement for birth registration at an Indian Consulate for those born abroad after December 10, 1992, is non-negotiable. The Court concurred, holding that consular registration is a mandatory prerequisite, not merely directory. It found that the petitioner failed to discharge the burden of proving Indian citizenship by birth or descent, thus finding no violation of her fundamental rights.

While dismissing the petition, the Court provided an avenue for the petitioner to pursue alternative remedies, allowing her to apply for Indian citizenship under Sections 5 or 6 of the Citizenship Act, 1955. The judgment reinforces that statutory compliance, particularly regarding citizenship by descent requirements, remains paramount, even when faced with sympathetic individual circumstances.

title: Kerala High Court Clarifies: No Embassy NOC Required for Inter-Nationality Marriages under Special Marriage Act
description:
The Kerala High Court has delivered a significant ruling, asserting that a No Objection Certificate (NOC) from a foreign embassy is not a mandatory prerequisite for solemnizing marriages between an Indian citizen and a foreign national under the Special Marriage Act, 1954. This judgment reinforces individual autonomy and clarifies statutory interpretation.

The case, Vinu Vikraman v. State of Kerala and Anr., arose when a Sub-Registrar insisted on an NOC from the Sri Lankan Embassy for an Indian citizen intending to marry a Sri Lankan woman. Challenging this demand, the petitioner approached the High Court. Justice Easwaran S. referred to the precedent of Saranya R. A. v. State of Kerala, highlighting that administrative authorities cannot impose extra-legal conditions not contemplated by the statute, as such demands arbitrarily restrict the fundamental right to marry.

The Court directed the Sub-Registrar to proceed with the marriage notice and solemnization without requiring the NOC. This decision is crucial in a globalized society, eliminating unnecessary procedural barriers and upholding principles of personal liberty, dignity, and equality. It provides a clear guiding precedent for Marriage Officers across India, ensuring smoother implementation of the Special Marriage Act.

title: The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026: A Significant Shift in India’s Insolvency Framework
description:
The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026, significantly reforms India's insolvency framework to enhance efficiency, reduce delays, and strengthen creditor participation. It addresses long-standing challenges like protracted admission processes, ineffective liquidation monitoring, and unresolved avoidance applications, reflecting a continuous evolution of the insolvency ecosystem.

The Act mandates Adjudicating Authorities (AA) to admit or reject insolvency applications (Sections 7, 9, 10) within 14 days, with written reasons for any delay, directly tackling prolonged admission processes. It restricts withdrawal of admitted applications post-Committee of Creditors (CoC) formation or resolution plan invitation, preventing misuse. The moratorium now expressly applies to sureties enforcing guarantees against the corporate debtor, clarifying previous ambiguities.

Crucially, the CoC's role is extended to supervise liquidation proceedings (Section 21(11)), a major departure from earlier frameworks. Provisions for avoidance transactions and fraudulent trading are strengthened, allowing proceedings to continue independently of CIRP or liquidation completion. Creditors can now independently approach the AA if Insolvency Professionals (IPs) fail to report such transactions, increasing IP accountability.

Liquidation reforms aim to expedite processes and improve recoveries, introducing mandatory pre-liquidation CoC consultations, promoting "going concern" sales, and allowing CoC-approved alternative resolution plans. Secured creditors must intimate the liquidator within 14 days to realize security, or it's deemed relinquished. For multiple secured creditors, independent enforcement requires 66% agreement. The CoC also gains power to replace liquidators, while former Resolution Professionals are barred from acting as liquidators, enhancing accountability and preventing conflicts.

A revolutionary addition is the "Creditor-Initiated Insolvency Resolution Process" (CIIRP). This new alternative pathway allows specified financial institutions to initiate insolvency with 51% financial creditor approval, after prior notice to the corporate debtor. Unlike traditional CIRP, the corporate debtor's management retains control under substantial IP supervision, aiming for faster, more flexible, and creditor-controlled resolution, inspired by debtor-in-possession models.

Overall, the 2026 amendment represents a comprehensive reform package designed to improve recovery outcomes and bolster creditor confidence. Its effective implementation, however, will largely hinge on supportive subordinate regulations, robust institutional capacity, and consistent judicial interpretation of these new provisions.

title: The Corporate Laws (Amendment) Bill, 2026: Reshaping India’s Corporate Governance and Compliance Landscape
description:
The Corporate Laws (Amendment) Bill, 2026 proposes significant changes to India's Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008 and Companies Act, 2013. The Bill aims to bolster governance standards, enhance ease of doing business, promote digitisation, and align Indian corporate laws with global financial practices. Key focus areas include International Financial Services Centres (IFSCs), corporate transparency, auditor accountability, digital corporate functioning, and the expansion of the National Financial Reporting Authority’s (NFRA) powers.

A major amendment formally recognises "Specified International Financial Services Centre LLPs," allowing them to maintain records, capital, and financial statements in permitted foreign currencies, significantly integrating them with global financial ecosystems. The Bill also makes the Companies Act's valuation framework applicable to LLPs and permits SEBI/IFSCA-regulated trusts to convert into LLPs. Similarly, companies in IFSCs can issue shares and maintain accounts in foreign currency.

The Bill advances digital governance by mandating company websites and formally recognising virtual or hybrid general meetings, while still requiring at least one physical AGM every three years. Share buy-back provisions are liberalised, offering companies greater flexibility in capital restructuring.

NFRA's powers are substantially strengthened, establishing it as a statutory body with perpetual succession. Its disciplinary and enforcement capabilities are expanded to include imposing heavy penalties, restricting auditing practices, and mandating auditor registration and periodic filings, with criminal consequences for non-compliance. This signals a stricter regulatory approach to financial reporting and audit oversight.

Corporate governance is enhanced with new board obligations for disclosing related-party transactions and regulatory impact. Independent director criteria are tightened, and restrictions on auditors providing non-audit services are extended to three years post-term to preserve independence.

The Bill also revises Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) applicability by increasing the net profit threshold for obligations to ₹10 crore and extending timelines for unspent funds. A significant policy shift involves decriminalising several procedural and technical non-compliances, replacing criminal punishments with monetary penalties to improve ease of doing business.

Overall, the Bill represents an extensive reform exercise, aiming to position India as a globally competitive financial services jurisdiction while simultaneously enforcing stricter financial reporting and audit oversight. Its enactment will necessitate a substantial overhaul of governance, compliance, and regulatory practices across companies, LLPs, and audit firms.

title: Intel Found in Breach of Indian Competition Law: CCI Imposes INR 27.38 Crore Penalty for India-Specific Warranty Restriction on Boxed Microprocessors
description:
The Competition Commission of India (CCI) has penalized Intel Corporation INR 27.38 crore for abusing its dominant position in the Indian market for boxed microprocessors (BMPs) for desktop personal computers. The ruling, dated February 12, 2026, found Intel in contravention for eight years (April 2016 – April 2024) due to an India-specific warranty policy that discriminated against Indian consumers and foreclosed parallel importers.

Intel's policy restricted in-India warranty service exclusively to BMPs purchased through its authorized Indian distributors, denying service for genuine products bought overseas. The CCI defined the relevant market as BMPs for desktop PCs in India and found Intel consistently dominant. This policy was deemed an abuse of dominance, specifically: unfair and discriminatory (Section 4(2)(a)(i)); limiting market and consumer choice by compelling purchases from higher-priced authorized channels (Section 4(2)(b)(i)); and denying market access to parallel importers like Matrix Info Systems (Section 4(2)(c)), which subsequently exited the market.

The CCI rejected Intel's defenses, including arguments distinguishing warranty from service, citing irrelevant precedents, questioning the informant's integrity, and claiming a de minimis effect. The Commission emphasized that the competitive effects, not individual grievances or limited direct claims, necessitate scrutiny for dominant entities.

The penalty of 8% of Intel's relevant turnover was based on the eight-year duration of the contravention, mitigated by Intel's voluntary discontinuation of the policy in April 2024 and cooperation during the investigation. The ruling mandates Intel to widely publicize this policy withdrawal. This decision establishes that dominant manufacturers cannot deploy geographically differentiated after-sales service policies to insulate authorized distribution networks from parallel import competition, thereby affirming the legitimacy of parallel importation and protecting consumer choice in technology markets.

title: Ready, Set, Innovate: How Sports Can Become India’s Next Intellectual Property Powerhouse
description:
India is repositioning sports as a powerhouse of innovation, manufacturing, and intellectual property (IP), moving beyond mere recreation. A recent government policy signals this shift, offering a three-year fee waiver for sports-related IP registrations (trademarks, patents, designs, copyrights, GIs) and launching a hackathon for smart wearables. This initiative encourages converting every aspect—from equipment and training devices to brands and local crafts—into valuable IP assets, fostering an IP-led sports economy.

This vision expands sports' future beyond athletes to include designers, engineers, manufacturers, students, and artisans. IP is crucial for converting sporting creativity into legally protected assets, transforming talent into enterprise. Examples range from GI-tagged regional products like the Kashmir willow bat, to advanced smart wearables and new sports formats. The goal is to connect grassroots participation and local production with global markets, following the principle to "innovate, patent, produce and prosper."

To achieve this, the policy advocates for sports manufacturing clusters, tech-driven solutions like wearables, and inclusive innovation involving startups and students. It emphasizes building robust infrastructure through CSR and promoting responsible endorsements to maintain market trust. Ultimately, India aims to become an "IP-enabled sports nation," shifting from being a passionate consumer to a global creator, manufacturer, and innovator in the sports ecosystem.

title: Bombay High Court Sets Aside Arbitral Award in Nayara Energy Dealership Dispute: Reaffirming Limits of Arbitral Powers in Commercial Contracts
description:
The Bombay High Court, in Nayara Energy Ltd. v. M/s. Mahendra Sales Services, has set aside an arbitral award, clarifying the scope of judicial review under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The judgment, delivered by Justice Sharmila U. Deshmukh, emphasizes that arbitral tribunals cannot introduce principles of equity or fairness into commercial contracts against their express terms. The dispute originated from a petrol pump dealership agreement and its termination.

The case involved Nayara Energy (formerly Essar Oil) and M/s. Mahendra Sales Services, whose proprietorship transitioned to Smt. Nanita Jain. Following alleged failure to meet sales targets, Nayara Energy terminated the 2006 franchise agreement in 2010 under Clause 14(b). Jain challenged this, citing substantial investments and lack of notice or opportunity of hearing. The arbitral tribunal, recognizing a valid contractual relationship with Jain based on party conduct, deemed the termination illegal for violating natural justice. It awarded Rs. 4 lakh compensation for machinery loss and notably, directed the restoration of the dealership.

Nayara Energy challenged the award before the High Court, arguing that the arbitrator erred by importing natural justice into a contract explicitly allowing termination without prior notice. They also contended that the compensation was unsupported by evidence and that the restoration of a determinable contract was illegal. The High Court upheld the arbitrator's finding of a contractual relationship with Nanita Jain. However, it critically ruled that the arbitrator acted with patent illegality by rewriting the contract based on equitable considerations, overriding express terms allowing immediate termination for breach. Furthermore, the Court held that restoration of a determinable contract, like a dealership, is impermissible under Section 14(1)(c) of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, with only damages being the appropriate remedy. The Rs. 4 lakh compensation was also found to be unsupported by pleadings and evidence.

Consequently, the Bombay High Court quashed the entire arbitral award, reaffirming the sanctity of contractual terms and limiting arbitral tribunals to the agreed legal framework. The decision underscores that arbitrators must adhere to substantive law and evidentiary requirements, not equitable considerations contrary to express contract provisions, and that determinable commercial contracts cannot be specifically performed. This ruling carries significant implications for future franchise and dealership disputes in India.

title: Timelines as Sacred: NCLAT Reinforces Mandatory Bar on Late Resolution Applicants in Ram Prasad Agarwala v. Bijay Murmuria
description:
The National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT), in Ram Prasad Agarwala v. Bijay Murmuria (29th April 2026), has affirmed the mandatory nature of timelines in corporate insolvency proceedings, setting aside an NCLT order that condoned a prospective resolution applicant's delayed Expression of Interest (EOI). This ruling underscores the sanctity of procedural compliance under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) and its Regulations.

The case involved Fairdeal Supplies Limited, where Respondent No. 5, Siraj Holding LLC, failed to submit its EOI by the stipulated deadline and was excluded from the provisional and final lists of prospective resolution applicants. The NCLT, however, allowed Respondent No. 5's application, condoning the delay in the interest of justice and stakeholders, enabling submission of a resolution plan.

The NCLAT's analysis hinged on Regulation 39(1)(b) of the CIRP Regulations, which explicitly states that a resolution plan "shall not" be considered from an applicant not included in the final list. The Tribunal interpreted "shall" as an imperative, leaving no discretion for the Committee of Creditors (CoC) or the Adjudicating Authority to entertain belated plans. It reiterated that only the CoC, under Regulation 36A, holds the exclusive power to modify or re-issue the EOI process, rejecting the NCLT's exercise of equitable jurisdiction as overriding express statutory prohibitions.

Consequently, the NCLAT allowed the appeal, setting aside the NCLT's order. It directed the CoC to consider resolution plans only from applicants already in the final list. Should the CoC deem fresh EOIs necessary, it must publish a new Form G. This judgment decisively reinforces the procedural integrity and time-bound objectives of the CIRP, guarding against ad hoc deviations and unprincipled application of equity.

title: NGT Orders and COVID Extensions in RERA Adjudication: The Karnataka REAT Ruling in Alekhya Property Developments v. Resmi Maream Vergis
description:
The Karnataka Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (REAT), in the case of Alekhya Property Developments Pvt. Ltd. v. Resmi Maream Vergis, delivered a judgment on March 26, 2026, modifying a Karnataka Real Estate Regulatory Authority (RERA) order. The ruling affirmed a promoter's liability for project delays while judiciously applying pandemic-induced extensions under the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.

Alekhya Property Developments, the promoter, failed to deliver possession of an apartment in the "Adarsh Lakefront Residential Phase-1" project by the contractual deadline of June 30, 2021, and did not obtain the Occupancy Certificate. Consequently, the allottees filed a complaint, leading the RERA Authority to order the promoter to pay Rs. 30,44,551 in delay compensation, calculated from June 30, 2021, to September 11, 2024 (date of soft handover).

The promoter appealed, arguing against the compensation. Key contentions included alleged payment delays by allottees, the impact of a 2016 NGT buffer zone order that was later clarified by the Supreme Court in March 2019, and entitlement to a nine-month COVID-19 extension. The allottees refuted these, stressing the promoter's non-compliance and the irrelevance of the NGT order as their agreement was signed after its resolution.

The REAT rejected the promoter's NGT defence, noting that the Agreement of Sale (November 16, 2019) was executed after the Supreme Court clarified the NGT order. Thus, the promoter could not claim delay concessions on this ground. However, the Tribunal upheld the COVID-19 extension, citing RERA circulars that granted a cumulative nine-month extension for projects with due dates on or after March 15, 2020. This effectively extended the project's completion deadline to March 30, 2022.

As a result, the REAT partially allowed the appeal, directing the promoter to pay delay interest from March 30, 2022, to September 11, 2024, thereby reducing the compensation period compared to the original RERA order. This judgment underscores that promoters are accountable for delays attributable to their own deficiencies but are entitled to statutory force majeure extensions, ensuring a balanced and equitable application of RERA provisions.

title: FSSAI Proposes Plastic-Free Packaging Mandate for Pan Masala: An Analysis of the Draft Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Amendment Regulations, 2026
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has issued a draft notification for the Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Amendment Regulations, 2026, proposing a mandate for plastic-free packaging of pan masala. This significant regulatory intervention aims to align food safety standards with environmental protection efforts, specifically targeting plastic waste reduction.

The core of the amendment introduces a new entry into Schedule IV of the existing Packaging Regulations, 2018. It stipulates that pan masala must be packaged exclusively in paper, paperboard, cellulose, or other naturally derived materials. The proposal explicitly prohibits the use of any plastic, synthetic polymers, copolymers, laminates, aluminium foil, and metallized layers. Permitted alternatives include tin or glass containers. This initiative directly addresses India's commitment to sustainable development, tackling the significant plastic waste generated by conventional pan masala sachets.

Crucially, the draft harmonizes food safety law with environmental law by incorporating specific clauses from the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, which already ban plastic for pan masala. This integration strengthens enforcement mechanisms, eliminates ambiguities, and empowers food safety officers to impose penalties under both the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, and environmental legislation.

The proposed regulations present substantial challenges for the pan masala industry, necessitating significant capital investment in new machinery, sourcing alternative materials, and redesigning packaging formats to maintain product integrity without previously utilized barrier layers. A statutory 30-day period is open for stakeholders to submit objections or suggestions to the FSSAI, facilitating industry input on the practicability and enforceability of these new standards. Upon finalization, these regulations are poised to establish a precedent for environmentally sustainable packaging within the food sector.

title: F OFF! High Court Quashes Sexual Harassment FIR Against Employer Over Use of Abusive Language in Work Email
description:
The Punjab and Haryana High Court quashed a sexual harassment (Section 354-A IPC) FIR against a company director who used "f off" in a work email to a female employee. The court emphasized that not every instance of workplace incivility or offensive language meets the statutory threshold for criminal prosecution under gender-specific penal provisions.

The case involved Director Abhikshek Shah and a Business Manager. During an email exchange about the complainant's medical leave request just before a critical company event, Shah used the profane phrase. The complainant resigned the same day. Subsequently, legal notices were exchanged regarding contractual obligations and financial demands. Over four months later, and after the company issued a legal notice, the complainant filed an FIR under Section 354-A IPC, alleging harassment.

The petitioner argued the FIR was retaliatory, aiming to pressure him financially, and that the remark, though crude, lacked any sexual overtone, arising from a work dispute. The court, applying principles from Bhajan Lal, highlighted the significant delay in filing the FIR, its timing after contractual disputes arose, and its context within a professional disagreement. Justice Kirti Singh concluded that "f off," while uncouth, did not carry a sexual insinuation and was not directed at the complainant's modesty or sexuality.

The Court held that Section 354-A(1)(iv) specifically requires "sexually coloured remarks." A solitary instance of vulgar or abusive language, devoid of sexual intent or connotation, does not satisfy this criterion. The judgment distinguishes unprofessional conduct from criminal culpability, affirming that criminal law should not be used to settle civil or employment disputes unless the core elements of the offense are unequivocally met.

Consequently, the FIR and all related proceedings were quashed, with the petitioner directed to deposit Rs. 20,000 in a welfare fund. This decision clarifies that while respectful workplace conduct is crucial, Section 354-A targets sexual harassment, not all forms of offensive language or general workplace rudeness. It underscores the importance of adhering to clear statutory thresholds and utilizing appropriate forums for dispute resolution.

title: Supreme Court Affirms Reproductive Autonomy: Constitutional Right to Abortion Extends Beyond Statutory Limits
description:
The Supreme Court of India has delivered a landmark judgment, reaffirming that a woman's constitutional right to reproductive autonomy under Article 21 takes precedence over statutory restrictions of the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act (MTP Act), especially in cases involving minors and unwanted pregnancies. The decision emphasizes that constitutional courts must prioritize the welfare and decisional autonomy of the pregnant woman over procedural limitations, even when statutory remedies are unavailable.

The appeal arose from a distressing case concerning a 15-year-old minor with a 28-week pregnancy, who exhibited severe psychological distress, including two suicide attempts, and unequivocally expressed unwillingness to continue the pregnancy. The Delhi High Court had dismissed her petition for termination, citing advanced gestational age and an AIIMS report that found no major psychiatric disorder, despite noting her physical fitness for the procedure.

The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, asserting that reproductive autonomy entails every pregnant woman's intrinsic right to decide whether to undergo an abortion. It rejected the State's proposal to compel birth and adoption, deeming it constitutionally impermissible as it subordinates the pregnant woman's welfare to the interest of an unborn child. The Court affirmed that the absence of a statutory remedy under the MTP Act does not bar a constitutional remedy under Articles 226 and 32. It also decisively rejected arguments against late-term abortions based on foetal normalcy or the passage of time, recognizing various reasons for delayed presentation and highlighting that denying legal termination only pushes women towards unsafe means. The Court prioritized the minor's best interests and reproductive autonomy, acknowledging her profound distress despite the absence of a formal psychiatric diagnosis.

This decision represents a principled, rights-based approach, reinforcing constitutional commitments to dignity, liberty, and personal autonomy. It provides authoritative guidance for future litigation involving late-term abortions, particularly for minors and vulnerable women, and underscores the imperative for aligning statutory frameworks with constitutional rights to ensure accessible, timely, and compassionate reproductive healthcare services.

title: Supreme Court Clarifies Writ Jurisdiction in Co-operative Disputes: A Landmark Ruling on Rajasthan’s Dairy Co-operatives
description:
The Supreme Court, in Ram Chandra Choudhary & Ors. v. Roop Nagar Dugdh Utpadak Sahakari Samiti Limited & Ors. (10 April 2026), delivered a landmark ruling clarifying the limits of Article 226 writ jurisdiction in co-operative society matters.
